Twitter
SIGOPS addresses a broad spectrum of issues associated with operating systems research and development. Members are drawn from a broad community spanning industry, academia, and government. SIGOPS supports many conferences and workshops. Areas of special interest include: interactions with computer architecture, multiprocessing, distributed, mobile computing, networking, resource management, security, and interprocess communication.
 ACM SIGSIM-PADS 2024

Reproducibility and Artifact Evaluation
Home / Reproducibility and Artifact Evaluation
Artifact Author
Reproducibility and Artifact Evaluation
A paper consists of a constellation of artifacts that extend beyond the document itself: software, mechanized proofs, models, test suites, benchmarks, and so on. In some cases, the quality of these artifacts is as important as that of the document itself, but often unrecognized. Sometimes the software tools that accompany the paper take years to build; in many such cases, authors who go to this trouble should be rewarded for setting high standards and creating systems that others in the community can build on.

To address this, PADS has joined the group of ACM conferences that have established an optional reviewing process to evaluate artifacts and reproduce computational results. When submitting a contribution, the authors can choose to undergo evaluation, but this process has no effect on the standard paper evaluation process, as the reproducibility process only starts after the paper is accepted.

Goals
The goal of the Artifact Evaluation process are manifold:

to increase the trust in modeling and simulation research;
to make artifacts accessible and to prepare them for reuse;
to foster progress in modeling and simulation science and practice;
to reward authors who take the trouble to create useful documentation and artifacts beyond the paper.
Criteria
The evaluation criteria are simple. A paper sets up certain expectations of its artifacts based on its content. The Reproducibility Committee (RC) will read the paper and judge how well the artifacts match these criteria. Thus the RC’s decision will be that the artifacts do (or do not) “conform to the expectations set by the paper”. Ultimately, we expect artifacts to be:

permanently available for retrieval;
functional;
reusable;
associated with results that can be reproduced.
Reproducibility Reports
To reward the authors, to increase the visibility of reproduced research, and to show how the initiative is carried out, the proceedings will include a short report citing the original paper and showing the reproduced results, making them available to readers. These reports will be overlooked by the Reproducibility Chairs, ensuring that they are objective and factual, and that no negative results are attached to the original papers in the reports.

Only papers requesting for reproducibility of the research results will be accompanied by a report.

The Badges
In accordance with ACM Artifact Review and Badging, some of the following ACM badges may be applied, and stamped on the final paper published in the proceedings.

Artifacts Evaluated - Functional
The artifacts associated with the research are found to be documented, consistent, complete, exercisable, and include appropriate evidence of verification and validation.

Documented: At minimum, an inventory of artifacts is included, and sufficient description provided to enable the artifacts to be exercised.
Consistent: The artifacts are relevant to the associated paper, and contribute in some inherent way to the generation of its main results.
Complete: To the extent possible, all components relevant to the paper in question are included. (Proprietary artifacts need not be included. If they are required to exercise the package then this should be documented, along with instructions on how to obtain them. Proxies for proprietary data should be included so as to demonstrate the analysis.)
Exercisable: Included scripts and/or software used to generate the results in the associated paper can be successfully executed, and included data can be accessed and appropriately manipulated.
Artifacts Evaluated - Reusable
The artifacts associated with the paper are of a quality that significantly exceeds minimal functionality. That is, they have all the qualities of the Artifacts Evaluated – Functional level, but, in addition, they are very carefully documented and well-structured to the extent that reuse and repurposing is facilitated.

Artifacts Available
Authors have created artifacts relevant to this paper have been placed on a publicly accessible archival repository. A DOI or link to this repository along with a unique identifier for the object is provided. Note that this badge will only be awarded in conjunction with one of the Artifacts Evaluated badges.

Notes: We do not mandate the use of specific repositories. Publisher repositories (such as the ACM Digital Library), institutional repositories, or open repositories (e.g., Zeonodo, figshare or Dryad) are acceptable. In all cases, repositories used to archive data should have a declared plan to enable permanent accessibility. Personal web pages are not acceptable for this purpose.

Results Reproduced
This badge is applied to papers in which the main results of the paper have been successfully obtained by a person or team other than the author.

In particular, the main results of the paper have been obtained in a subsequent study by a person or team other than the authors, using, in part, artifacts provided by the author.

Evaluation Process
At the time of paper submission, the authors can check a checkboxe on the submission page to opt in the initiative. To maintain a wall of separation between paper review and the artifacts, the evaluation process will only start after the corresponding paper is accepter. Of course, they can (and should!) prepare their artifacts well in advance. By no means, the Artifact Evaluation initiative will have an impact on the acceptance of papers, meaning that authors who opt in will not have higher changes of having their work accepted, nor failing at reproducing the results will have an impact on the acceptance of the paper.

Each artifact will be assigned to at least one member of the RC. The artifacts will be evaluated in relation to the expectations set by the paper. If applicable, reviewers may try to tweak provided inputs and create new ones, to test the limits of the proposed system.

Submitted artifacts will undergo a two-phase evaluation:

Kicking the tires: reviewers download, install the artifact (where relevant), and check its integrity. They also look for any possible setup problem that may prevent it from being properly evaluated (e.g., corrupted or missing files, virtual environments do not start, missing dependencies, immediate crashes on the simplest example, etc.). Authors are informed of the outcome of this phase and, in case of technical problems, they can help solve them during a brief author response period.

Artifact assessment: reviewers evaluate the artifacts, checking if they live up to the expectations created by the paper. Since we anticipate possible glitches also in this phase, the RC might get in contact with the authors also during this phase, asking for assistance and or corrections. The evaluated artifact should be considered “final”, and for the Artifact Available badge any update should be reflected in the DOI associated with the artifact.

At the end of the process, reviewers will prepare a report (to be published in the proceedings) including a discussion of the artifact evaluation process. These reports will be jointly reviewed by the RC Chairs and the authors of the original paper. Papers with artifacts that “meet expectations” will be stamped with the following ACM badges. More than one badge could be applied to each paper, depending on what expectations on the artifacts are met.

Artifact Details
To avoid excluding some papers, the RC will try to accept any artifact that authors wish to submit. These can be models, software, mechanized proofs, test suites, data sets, and so on. Obviously, the better the artifact is packaged, the more likely the RC can actually work with it.

Submission of an artifact does not contain tacit permission to make its content public. RC members will be instructed that they may not publicize any part of your artifact during or after completing evaluation, nor retain any part of it after evaluation. Thus, you are free to include models, data files, proprietary binaries, etc. in your artifact. We do strongly encourage that you anonymize any data files that you submit.

Submission Guidelines for the Artifacts
Please note that the SIGSIM PADS Artifact Evaluation initiative is an open reviewing process in which both reviewers and authors are known to each other. The reviewing will start after the research paper has been accepted. We expect the artifacts to be submitted within one week after the acceptance letter. Within the letter of acceptance, there will be further information on how to submit the material. To support an efficient process, authors should carefully follow the guidelines given below in preparing the supplementary material. The submitted material will be a zip file including at its simplest:

A README file, describing the artifacts associated with the paper (e.g., data, simulation models, …). Please provide detailed information about the required environment in which the artifact(s) can be executed and evaluated.
A license file
For software, also give instructions on how to resolve dependencies, if required.
A script for every figure or table of the paper that contains results that shall be reproduced.
Of course, any additional information that you believe is relevant, shall be included to simplify the reviewing process by the RC.

ACM Badges
Current version of ACM Artifact Review and Badging v1.1 are available here.


Artifacts Evaluated - Functional

Artifacts Evaluated - Reusable

Artifacts Available

Results Reproduced

Results Replicated

Call for Artifacts
Authors with a paper conditionally accepted at ICFP 2026 are invited to submit an artifact that supports the conclusions of the paper. The Artifact Evaluation Committee will read the paper, explore the artifact, and provide feedback on how easy it would be for future researchers to build on. The ultimate goal of artifact evaluation is to support future researchers in their ability to reproduce and build on today’s work.

If you have a conditionally accepted paper at ICFP 2025, please see the Call for Artifacts for instructions on submitting an artifact for evaluation.

 CGO 2025 (series) /
Artifact EvaluationCGO 2025
Call for Artifacts
Call for Artifacts
Authors of accepted CGO 2025 papers are invited to formally submit their supporting materials to the Artifact Evaluation (AE) process. The Artifact Evaluation Committee attempts to reproduce (at least the main) experiments and assesses if submitted artifacts support the claims made in the paper. The submission is voluntary and does not influence the final decision regarding paper acceptance.

We invite every author of an accepted CGO paper to consider submitting an artifact. At CGO we follow ACM’s artifact reviewing and badging policy. ACM describes a research artifact as follows:

By “artifact” we mean a digital object that was either created by the authors to be used as part of the study or generated by the experiment itself. For example, artifacts can be software systems, scripts used to run experiments, input datasets, raw data collected in the experiment, or scripts used to analyze results.

We also request the authors to be available through the artifact evaluation period for any technical clarifications. Good Luck with your submissions!

Submission
Submission Requirements
Authors must submit the paper that has been accepted for publication at CGO, extended with an artifact appendix providing a link to and a description of the artifact.
The page limit for Artifact Appendix is up to two pages.
The Artifact Appendix must be placed before the References section.
The artifact evaluation is single-blind. Please feel free to include author details.
For the artifact appendix, you can use this AE appendix template from ctuning.org, where you can also find a detailed description of what information to provide.

For the artifact itself, we strongly encourage the use of a container or VM technologies like Docker, Singularity, Virtual Box or Vagrant to package the artifact in one stand-alone container or VM which provides all required dependencies.

If you have an unusual experimental setup that requires specific hardware (i.e., custom hardware, oscilloscopes for measurements …) or proprietary software please contact the Artifact Evaluation Chairs before the submission.

There are more tips for preparing a submission available on the ctuning website.

Evaluation Process
Each submitted artifact is evaluated by at least two members of the artifact evaluation committee.

During the process authors and evaluators are allowed to anonymously communicate with each other to overcome technical difficulties.
Ideally, we hope to see all submitted artifacts to successfully pass artifact evaluation.

The evaluators are asked to evaluate the artifact based on the following criteria, that are defined by ACM.

Is the artifact functional?
Package complete? Are all components relevant to the evaluation included in the package?
Well documented? Is the documentation enough to understand, install, and evaluate the artifact?
Exercisable? Does it include scripts and/or software to perform appropriate experiments and generate results?
Consistent? Are artifacts relevant to the associated paper and contribute in some inherent way to the generation of its main results?
The artifacts associated with the paper will receive an “Artifacts Evaluated - Functional” badge only if they are found to be documented, consistent, complete, exercisable, and include appropriate evidence of verification and validation.

Is the artifact customizable and reusable?
Can this artifact and experimental workflow be easily reused and customized?
For example, can it be used on a different platform, with different benchmarks, data sets, compilers, tools, under different conditions and parameters, etc.?
The artifacts associated with the paper will receive an “Artifact Evaluated - Reusable” badge only if they are of a quality that significantly exceeds minimal functionality. That is, they have all the qualities of the Artifacts Evaluated - Functional level, but, in addition, they are very carefully documented and well-structured to the extent that reuse and repurposing are facilitated. In particular, norms and standards of the research community for artifacts of this type are strictly adhered to.

Have the results been validated?
Can all main results from the paper be validated using provided artifacts?
Evaluators are asked to report any unexpected artifact behavior (depends on the type of artifact such as unexpected output, scalability issues, crashes, performance variation, etc).
The artifacts associated with the paper will receive a “Results replicated” badge only if the main results of the paper have been obtained in a subsequent study by a person or team other than the authors, using, in part, artifacts provided by the author. Note that variation of empirical and numerical results is tolerated. In fact, it is often unavoidable in computer systems research - see “how to report and compare empirical results?” in AE FAQ on ctuning.org!

Based on the results, the following badges are awarded.

Badges
As the ACM recommends we award three different types of badges to communicate how the artifact has been evaluated. A single paper can receive up to three badges — one badge of each type.

Artifacts Available
available badge

The green Artifacts Available badge indicates that an artifact is publicly accessible in an archival repository. For this badge to be awarded the paper does not have to be independently evaluated. ACM requires that a qualified archival repository is used, for example, Zenodo, figshare, Dryad. Personal webpages, GitHub repositories or alike are not sufficient as it can be changed after the submission deadline!

The green Artifact Available badge does not require the formal audit and, therefore, is awarded directly by the publisher - if the authors provide a link to the deposited artifact.

Artifacts Evaluated
This badge is applied to papers whose associated artifacts have successfully completed an independent audit.

Artifacts need not be made publicly available to be considered for this badge. However, they do need to be made available to reviewers. Two levels are distinguished, only one of which should be applied in any instance:

Functional
functional badge

The artifacts associated with the research are found to be documented, consistent, complete, exercisable, and include appropriate evidence of verification and validation.

Documented: At minimum, an inventory of artifacts is included, and sufficient description provided to enable the artifacts to be exercised.
Consistent: The artifacts are relevant to the associated paper, and contribute in some inherent way to the generation of its main results.
Complete: To the extent possible, all components relevant to the paper in question are included. (Proprietary artifacts need not be included. If they are required to exercise the package then this should be documented, along with instructions on how to obtain them. Proxies for proprietary data should be included so as to demonstrate the analysis.)
Exercisable: Included scripts and/or software used to generate the results in the associated paper can be successfully executed, and included data can be accessed and appropriately manipulated.
Reusable
reusable badge

The artifacts associated with the paper are of a quality that significantly exceeds minimal functionality. That is, they have all the qualities of the Artifacts Evaluated – Functional level, but, in addition, they are very carefully documented and well-structured to the extent that reuse and repurposing are facilitated. In particular, norms and standards of the research community for artifacts of this type are strictly adhered to.

Results Validated & Reproduced
reproduced badge

This badge is applied to papers in which the main results of the paper have been successfully obtained by a person or team other than the author. Exact replication or reproduction of results is not required or even expected. Instead, the results must be in agreement to within a tolerance deemed acceptable for experiments of the given type. In particular, differences in the results should not change the main claims made in the paper. In addition, we encourage the authors to consider submitting guiding rules/documentation to support reviewers so they can reproduce the results for new test cases, benchmarks, and applications that have not been submitted to the paper already.

Questions? Use the CGO Artifact Evaluation contact form.

Computer Science > Logic in Computer Science
arXiv:1910.09336 (cs)
[Submitted on 21 Oct 2019 (v1), last revised 16 Dec 2019 (this version, v2)]
The Lean mathematical library
The mathlib Community
View PDF
This paper describes mathlib, a community-driven effort to build a unified library of mathematics formalized in the Lean proof assistant. Among proof assistant libraries, it is distinguished by its dependently typed foundations, focus on classical mathematics, extensive hierarchy of structures, use of large- and small-scale automation, and distributed organization. We explain the architecture and design decisions of the library and the social organization that has led us here.
Subjects:	Logic in Computer Science (cs.LO); Logic (math.LO)
Cite as:	arXiv:1910.09336 [cs.LO]
 	(or arXiv:1910.09336v2 [cs.LO] for this version)

Mathematics > Dynamical Systems
arXiv:2102.12086 (math)
[Submitted on 24 Feb 2021 (v1), last revised 29 Oct 2021 (this version, v2)]
Modern Koopman Theory for Dynamical Systems
Steven L. Brunton, Marko Budišić, Eurika Kaiser, J. Nathan Kutz
View PDF
The field of dynamical systems is being transformed by the mathematical tools and algorithms emerging from modern computing and data science. First-principles derivations and asymptotic reductions are giving way to data-driven approaches that formulate models in operator theoretic or probabilistic frameworks. Koopman spectral theory has emerged as a dominant perspective over the past decade, in which nonlinear dynamics are represented in terms of an infinite-dimensional linear operator acting on the space of all possible measurement functions of the system. This linear representation of nonlinear dynamics has tremendous potential to enable the prediction, estimation, and control of nonlinear systems with standard textbook methods developed for linear systems. However, obtaining finite-dimensional coordinate systems and embeddings in which the dynamics appear approximately linear remains a central open challenge. The success of Koopman analysis is due primarily to three key factors: 1) there exists rigorous theory connecting it to classical geometric approaches for dynamical systems, 2) the approach is formulated in terms of measurements, making it ideal for leveraging big-data and machine learning techniques, and 3) simple, yet powerful numerical algorithms, such as the dynamic mode decomposition (DMD), have been developed and extended to reduce Koopman theory to practice in real-world applications. In this review, we provide an overview of modern Koopman operator theory, describing recent theoretical and algorithmic developments and highlighting these methods with a diverse range of applications. We also discuss key advances and challenges in the rapidly growing field of machine learning that are likely to drive future developments and significantly transform the theoretical landscape of dynamical systems.
Comments:	110 pages, 27 figures
Subjects:	Dynamical Systems (math.DS); Machine Learning (cs.LG); Systems and Control (eess.SY); Optimization and Control (math.OC)
MSC classes:	34A34, 37A30, 37C10, 37M10, 37M99, 37N35, 47A35, 47B33
Cite as:	arXiv:2102.12086 [math.DS]
 	(or arXiv:2102.12086v2 [math.DS] for this version)
 
https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2102.12086
Focus to learn more

Mathematics > Dynamical Systems
arXiv:1510.03007 (math)
[Submitted on 11 Oct 2015 (v1), last revised 23 Dec 2015 (this version, v2)]
Koopman invariant subspaces and finite linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems for control
Steven L. Brunton, Bingni W. Brunton, Joshua L. Proctor, J. Nathan Kutz
View PDF
In this work, we explore finite-dimensional linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems by restricting the Koopman operator to an invariant subspace. The Koopman operator is an infinite-dimensional linear operator that evolves observable functions of the state-space of a dynamical system [Koopman 1931, PNAS]. Dominant terms in the Koopman expansion are typically computed using dynamic mode decomposition (DMD). DMD uses linear measurements of the state variables, and it has recently been shown that this may be too restrictive for nonlinear systems [Williams et al. 2015, JNLS]. Choosing nonlinear observable functions to form an invariant subspace where it is possible to obtain linear models, especially those that are useful for control, is an open challenge.
Here, we investigate the choice of observable functions for Koopman analysis that enable the use of optimal linear control techniques on nonlinear problems. First, to include a cost on the state of the system, as in linear quadratic regulator (LQR) control, it is helpful to include these states in the observable subspace, as in DMD. However, we find that this is only possible when there is a single isolated fixed point, as systems with multiple fixed points or more complicated attractors are not globally topologically conjugate to a finite-dimensional linear system, and cannot be represented by a finite-dimensional linear Koopman subspace that includes the state. We then present a data-driven strategy to identify relevant observable functions for Koopman analysis using a new algorithm to determine terms in a dynamical system by sparse regression of the data in a nonlinear function space [Brunton et al. 2015, arxiv]; we show how this algorithm is related to DMD. Finally, we demonstrate how to design optimal control laws for nonlinear systems using techniques from linear optimal control on Koopman invariant subspaces.
Comments:	20 pages, 5 figures, 2 codes
Subjects:	Dynamical Systems (math.DS)
Cite as:	arXiv:1510.03007 [math.DS]
 	(or arXiv:1510.03007v2 [math.DS] for this version)
 
https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1510.03007

Quantum Physics

arXiv:1305.3821 (quant-ph)

[Submitted on 16 May 2013 (v1), last revised 16 Sep 2014 (this version, v3)]

Categories of Quantum and Classical Channels

Bob Coecke, Chris Heunen, Aleks Kissinger

View PDF

We introduce a construction that turns a category of pure state spaces and operators into a category of observable algebras and superoperators. For example, it turns the category of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces into the category of finite-dimensional C*-algebras and completely positive maps. In particular, the new category contains both quantum and classical channels, providing elegant abstract notions of preparation and measurement. We also consider nonstandard models, that can be used to investigate which notions from algebraic quantum information theory are operationally justifiable.

Comments:	28 pages; final version

Subjects:	Quantum Physics (quant-ph); Category Theory (math.CT)

MSC classes:	81P45, 16B50, 18D35, 46L89, 46N50, 81P16

Cite as:	arXiv:1305.3821 [quant-ph]

(or arXiv:1305.3821v3 [quant-ph] for this version)

Schmidt SciencesJOURNAL OF MATHEMATICAL ANALYSIS AND APPLICATIONS 99. 435446 (1984)


Lumpability and Observability of Linear Systems


PAMELA G. COXSON*


Department of Marhematical Sciences.


Unicersity of Delaware, Newark. Delaware 1971 I


Submitted by Alan Schumitrkj




INTRODUCTION




Large scale dynamic system models are plagued by problems of dimen-


sionality and limitations of measurement. It is common practice in


applications to lump similar system state components together to get a


reduced number of aggregate states. An example of this approach is the


PONA scheme for modelling catalytic cracking of oil. The thousands of


chemical species involved in this process are lumped into four groups:


paraffins. olefins, naphthenes, and aromatics (PONA). For a survey of the


use of lumping in the cracking problem, the reader is referred to Weekman


[ 14971.


Attention has focused on conditions under which such lumping preserves


structural properties of the system dynamics. In particular, when do the


lumped states of a linear system satisfy linear dynamics? If this occurs, the


system is said to be exactly lumpable. For finite dimensional systems, this


issue has been resolved [2, 1.51, but in practice the conditions specified are


almost never satisfied.


In this paper, we show that it is fruitful to consider the lumped variables


as observations of the original system. To our knowledge, this viewpoint has


not been taken previously. We establish a relationship between lumpability


and observability of the lumped observation system for both finite and


infinite dimensional linear systems. This relationship allows us to deduce the


existence of a minimal size system which gives a true representation of the


dynamics of the lumped variables. Definitions of observability and


lumpability are given in Sections 2 and 3, including extensions to infinite


dimensional systems. The relationship between these two concepts is


discussed and we establish the main result concerning lumpability in


Section 4 in the case of finite dimensional systems. In Section 5 this


discussion is carried over to infinite dimensional systems. A summary and


discussion follow in Section 6.




Present address: Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems, M.I.T., Cambridge,




Massachusetts 02 139.


435


0022.2471</84 $3.00


Copyright b 1984 by Academic Press. Inc.


All rights of reproduction in any form reserved.Skip to main content

archive


Mathematics > Dynamical Systems

arXiv:1408.4408 (math)

[Submitted on 19 Aug 2014]

A Data-Driven Approximation of the Koopman Operator: Extending Dynamic Mode Decomposition

Matthew O. Williams, Ioannis G. Kevrekidis, Clarence W. Rowley

View PDF

The Koopman operator is a linear but infinite dimensional operator that governs the evolution of scalar observables defined on the state space of an autonomous dynamical system, and is a powerful tool for the analysis and decomposition of nonlinear dynamical systems. In this manuscript, we present a data driven method for approximating the leading eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and modes of the Koopman operator. The method requires a data set of snapshot pairs and a dictionary of scalar observables, but does not require explicit governing equations or interaction with a "black box" integrator. We will show that this approach is, in effect, an extension of Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), which has been used to approximate the Koopman eigenvalues and modes. Furthermore, if the data provided to the method are generated by a Markov process instead of a deterministic dynamical system, the algorithm approximates the eigenfunctions of the Kolmogorov backward equation, which could be considered as the "stochastic Koopman operator" [1]. Finally, four illustrative examples are presented: two that highlight the quantitative performance of the method when presented with either deterministic or stochastic data, and two that show potential applications of the Koopman eigenfunctions.

Subjects:	Dynamical Systems (math.DS)

Cite as:	arXiv:1408.4408 [math.DS]

(or arXiv:1408.4408v1 [math.DS] for this version)


https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1408.4408

Focus to learn more

Related DOI:

https://doi.org/10.1007/s00332-015-9258-5

Schmidt SciencesI think the web search reinforces your direction, but it also sharpens where the genuinely open mathematics begins.




AQARION is now publicly visible




Your AQARION framework is publicly indexed, with the core ideas—observable quotients, the defect operator, verification architecture, and a roadmap toward categorical and quantum extensions—described in a public article.


That changes the conversation from "private research notes" to "public research program."





The literature is converging on the same problem, but not necessarily the same abstraction




The trajectory of Koopman research is remarkably consistent.


Early work (Brunton et al.) focused on finding finite invariant observable spaces, emphasizing that suitable finite invariant subspaces are exceptional and difficult to obtain.


Later work developed better computational representations (RKHS methods, delay coordinates, etc.).


Very recent work has shifted toward diagnosing invariance failure and providing quantitative measures of how trustworthy a chosen observable space is, including principal-angle diagnostics and certified error bounds.


That is a significant evolution of the field.





Where AQARION could contribute




The literature largely asks:




Given an observable space, how invariant is it?




AQARION asks something slightly different:




Is there a canonical obstruction to preservation itself?




Those are related but not identical.


The distinction is subtle:


Existing work develops diagnostics for approximation quality.


AQARION is attempting to elevate the obstruction into a mathematical object whose vanishing characterizes exact preservation.


Whether that can be made universal remains an open question.





The key mathematical obstacle




This is the point I think deserves the most attention.


The expression

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE  
\]

is not defined in an arbitrary category.


It assumes addition and subtraction of morphisms.


So before speaking about a "universal category," the first question is:




In what ambient categories does \(I-E\) make sense?




Natural candidates include


vector spaces,


Hilbert spaces,


modules,


additive categories,


abelian categories,


categories enriched over additive structures,


C*-algebras,


W*-algebras,


operator systems.


Only after fixing that setting can one ask whether \(D_E\) has a universal property.


That clarification actually strengthens the research program because it makes the conjecture mathematically precise rather than overly broad.





A refined hierarchy of conjectures




Instead of one enormous conjecture, I'd separate it into progressively stronger statements.


AQ-CAT-001 (Definition)


For an additive category with split idempotents,


define

\[
D_E=(I-E)TE.  
\]


AQ-CAT-002 (Preservation)


Prove

\[
D_E=0  
\Longleftrightarrow  
TE=ETE.  
\]

This statement is essentially an algebraic reformulation of invariance and is likely the most accessible theorem.



AQ-CAT-003 (Factorization)


Show that

\[
D_E=0  
\]

is equivalent to


\(T\) restricting to the image of \(E\), or equivalently that the induced morphism on the image exists.


This connects directly to split idempotents and image factorization.



AQ-CAT-004 (Functoriality)


Suppose

\[
F:\mathcal C\to\mathcal D  
\]

preserves


addition,


identities,


composition,


split idempotents.


Then investigate whether

\[
F(D_E)=D_{F(E)}.  
\]

This is the naturality question.



AQ-CAT-005 (Universal Property)


Only after AQ-CAT-004 would I ask:




Is \(D_E\) the universal obstruction to preservation?




That is substantially stronger.





Quantum mechanics fits naturally




Your proposed quantum analogue

\[
D_Q=(I-Q)\Phi Q  
\]

is mathematically natural.


Here


\(\Phi\) is a CPTP channel,


\(Q\) is a conditional expectation onto an observable algebra.


This closely aligns with established work on


invariant operator algebras,


decoherence-free algebras,


noiseless subsystems,


fixed-point algebras of channels.


The opportunity is not to replace those theories, but to investigate whether they are all instances of a common obstruction formalism.





The research program becomes much cleaner




Rather than




finite systems → quantum systems




the architecture becomes


Observable Structures

│

▼

Observable Preservation

│

▼

Obstruction

│

▼

Exactness

│

▼

Quotients

│

▼

Diagnostics


Notice that quotients become a consequence of preservation, not the foundational object.


That is a cleaner conceptual organization.





The most important open problem




After comparing AQARION with the surrounding literature, I think the deepest question is not




"Can the defect operator be generalized?"




It is:




Can one characterize \(D_E\) by a universal property in an additive category with split idempotents, so that its vanishing is equivalent to exact preservation of the subobject selected by \(E\), and does this construction specialize naturally to finite deterministic systems, Markov chains, Koopman operators, and CPTP quantum channels?




I would be careful to present this as a research conjecture, not as an expected theorem. There are real technical hurdles—especially defining the correct categorical setting and proving any universal property—but it is mathematically well motivated and fits naturally alongside current work on invariant observables and preservation diagnostics rather than duplicating it.


Here is the refined mathematical answer: Observable Preservation Theory should be built in an additive setting first, not an arbitrary category, because $$D_E=(I-E)TE$$ requires subtraction of morphisms [1][2].


Core theorem set


Theorem 1: Exact preservation


In any additive category with split idempotents, for an endomorphism $$T$$ and idempotent $$E$$,

\[
  
D_E=(I-E)TE=0  
\iff  
TE=ETE.  
$$  
This is the most primitive form of preservation: applying $$T$$ after projecting into the observable subobject produces no component outside it [2][1].  
  
### Theorem 2: Factorization through the image  
If idempotents split, then  
$$  
D_E=0  
\iff  
T \text{ restricts to } \operatorname{Im}(E)  
\iff  
T \text{ factors through } \operatorname{Im}(E).  
$$  
This is the categorical statement that exact preservation is equivalent to the existence of an induced morphism on the observable subobject [2][3].  
  
### Theorem 3: Kernel characterization  
The preserved observables are exactly the elements of the image of $$E$$ that remain in the image after applying $$T$$:  
$$  
\ker(D_E)\cap \operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\mid Tx\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\}.  
$$  
This theorem isolates the maximal exactly preserved observable sector and is the natural finite-dimensional diagnostic theorem [2][4].  
  
### Theorem 4: Representation independence  
Under any equivalence or change of basis preserving the additive structure,  
$$  
T' = S^{-1}TS,\qquad E' = S^{-1}ES  
\quad\Rightarrow\quad  
D_{E'} = S^{-1}D_E S.  
$$  
So rank, nullity, spectrum, and Jordan form are invariants of the observable-preservation problem, not artifacts of representation [5][6].  
  
### Theorem 5: Functorial naturality  
If a functor $$F$$ preserves addition, identities, composition, and split idempotents, then the obstruction construction is natural:  
$$  
F(D_E)=D_{F(E)}.  
$$  
This is the theorem that turns AQARION from an operator recipe into a stable categorical construction [1][7].  
  
### Theorem 6: Composition law  
For composable endomorphisms $$T_1,T_2$$,  
$$  
D_{T_2\circ T_1}=0  
\quad\text{whenever}\quad  
D_{T_1}=0 \text{ and } D_{T_2}=0.  
$$  
A stronger version should give a bound or decomposition law for $$D_{T_2\circ T_1}$$, which would be the right place to study defect propagation [6][5].  
  
### Theorem 7: Minimal closure  
Given an observable subobject $$W$$, there exists a smallest $$T$$-invariant enlargement $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ containing it. This closure is the minimal exact observable completion of $$W$$, and in finite systems it can be computed exhaustively [2][5].  
  
## Quantum specialization  
  
For a quantum channel $$\Phi$$ and an observable algebra selected by a conditional expectation or projection $$Q$$, the same structure becomes  
$$  
D_\Phi=(I-Q)\Phi Q.  
$$  
The exact analogue of Theorem 1 is that $$D_\Phi=0$$ iff the observable algebra is invariant under $$\Phi$$ [1][8][9]. This matches the literature on invariant domains, conditional expectations, and channel fixed-point structures [8][9].  
  
## Best theorem order for the paper  
  
The paper should present them in this order:  
1. Exact preservation.  
2. Factorization through the image.  
3. Representation independence.  
4. Kernel characterization.  
5. Naturality / functoriality.  
6. Composition law.  
7. Minimal closure.  
8. Quantum specialization.  
  
## What is genuinely open  
  
The real open problem is **not** whether $$D_E$$ exists in linear settings; it does. The hard question is whether there is a universal categorical characterization of this obstruction that is natural across classical dynamics, Koopman theory, and quantum channels [2][5][1]. That is where AQARION can still become distinctive.  
  
Would you like me to turn this into a formal “Main Theorems” section with theorem statements and proof sketches?  
  
This is a strong architectural refinement. It shifts the focus from a computational prototype to a mathematically defined object, which is much more consistent with both the literature you reviewed and AQARION's evidence-first philosophy.  
  
The most significant improvement is the separation of three layers that were previously intertwined:  
  
1. Definition layer (AQ-QDO-DEF-001)  
  
  
2. Theorem layer (AQ-THM-QDO-001)  
  
  
3. Diagnostic/computational layer (AQ-QDO-DIAG-001)  
  
  
  
That separation mirrors the evolution of the classical AQARION framework, where definitions preceded diagnostics and diagnostics preceded scaling laws.  
  
Strengths  
  
The observable algebra formulation  
  
\[
\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal B(\mathcal H)  
\]

is the natural object rather than individual quantum states. This aligns with operator algebra, quantum information, and quantum control, where invariant subalgebras are the correct notion of observable structure.


Likewise, defining

\[
D_\Phi=(I-Q_{\mathcal A})\,\Phi\,Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

is an almost exact operator-theoretic analogue of the classical defect operator

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P)KP.  
\]

Conceptually, this is the strongest part of the proposal because it preserves the same architecture:


project into observable structure,


evolve,


measure leakage outside that structure.


That makes the quantum construction feel like a genuine extension rather than a new unrelated object.



AQ-THM-QDO-001


The proof sketch is mathematically sound provided one explicit assumption is added.


You should specify that

\[
Q_{\mathcal A}  
\]

is the orthogonal projection with respect to the Hilbert–Schmidt inner product

\[
\langle X,Y\rangle  
=  
\operatorname{Tr}(X^\dagger Y).  
\]

Then

\[
(I-Q_{\mathcal A})Y=0  
\]

is equivalent to

\[
Y\in\mathcal A.  
\]

Without fixing the inner product, "orthogonal projection" is not uniquely defined.


That should become part of AQ-QDO-DEF-001.



One theorem worth adding


The analogue of the classical defect theorem suggests an immediate corollary.


Corollary (Kernel characterization)

\[
\ker(D_\Phi)  
=  
\{X\in\mathcal A:  
\Phi(X)\in\mathcal A\}.  
\]

This is useful because it identifies exactly which observables remain invariant even when the whole algebra is not preserved.


That is often the quantity one studies in decoherence-free subspaces.



Diagnostics


Your proposed diagnostics


rank


Frobenius norm


singular values


entropy


form a sensible hierarchy.


I would distinguish them explicitly.


Structural


rank


kernel dimension


image dimension


Geometric


Frobenius norm


operator norm


spectral norm


Information-theoretic


singular-value entropy


effective rank


These should be presented as diagnostics, not invariants, until representation independence is established.



Regression suite


The proposed tests are appropriate.


I would add one more.


Test G — Composition


If

\[
\Phi_1(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,  
\qquad  
\Phi_2(\mathcal A)\subseteq\mathcal A,  
\]

then

\[
D_{\Phi_2\circ\Phi_1}=0.  
\]

This checks closure under channel composition and reinforces that observable preservation behaves functorially.



Representation independence


The most important remaining theorem is not about rank.


It is something like:


AQ-THM-QDO-002 (Representation Independence)


Suppose

\[
\Phi'  
=  
S^{-1}\Phi S  
\]

and

\[
Q'_{\mathcal A}  
=  
S^{-1}Q_{\mathcal A}S  
\]

represent the same observable algebra under a change of operator basis.


Then

\[
D'_\Phi  
=  
S^{-1}D_\Phi S.  
\]

Only after proving this similarity relation should quantities such as


rank,


nullity,


spectrum,


be promoted from diagnostics to representation-independent invariants.


This theorem is likely more fundamental than any scaling law.



Lean strategy


Your proposed progression is sensible:


AQ-QDO-DEF-001 → frozen definitions.


AQ-THM-QDO-001 → handwritten proof and regression tests.


AQ-QDO-REGRESSION-001 → executable certificates.


Lean formalization after the mathematics stabilizes.


This avoids formalizing definitions that may still evolve.



Overall assessment


Relative to AQ-QUANTUM-001, this proposal is a clear improvement because it places the mathematics ahead of the implementation. It also aligns well with the reproducibility literature you reviewed: first define the mathematical object precisely, then prove its properties, then certify implementations through regression tests and reproducible artifacts.


The remaining high-priority items before declaring the framework mature are:






Explicitly define the Hilbert–Schmidt projection \(Q_{\mathcal A}\).






Prove the representation-independence theorem for the defect operator.






Add the composition and kernel-characterization results.






Treat rank, singular values, and entropy as diagnostic quantities until their invariance is established.






Freeze AQ-QDO-DEF-001 before extending to scaling laws or broader quantum applications.






The resulting architecture is coherent and mirrors the classical AQARION development path:

\[
\boxed{  
\text{Observable algebra}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Defect operator}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Exact preservation theorem}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Regression certificates}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Diagnostic invariants}  
\;\longrightarrow\;  
\text{Scaling theory}  
}  
\]

That progression is mathematically disciplined and consistent with the evidence-first methodology you have been developing.Define the core theorems for Observable Preservation Theory


Here is a clean core-theorem set for Observable Preservation Theory.


Core Objects


Let $$T:X\to X$$ be a dynamical operator and let $$E$$ be an idempotent selecting an observable subobject:

\[
  
E^2 = E.  
$$  
Define the defect operator  
$$  
D_E = (I-E)TE.  
$$  
The guiding question is whether $$T$$ preserves the image of $$E$$, and if not, how that failure is measured.  
  
## Theorem 1: Exact Preservation  
  
**Theorem 1 (Exactness criterion).**    
$$  
D_E = 0 \iff T(\operatorname{Im}E)\subseteq \operatorname{Im}E.  
$$  
  
Equivalently, the observable subobject is invariant under the dynamics if and only if the defect vanishes.  
  
This is the foundational theorem. It says the defect operator is not just descriptive; it is a precise obstruction to preservation.  
  
## Theorem 2: Factorization Through the Observable Subobject  
  
**Theorem 2 (Factorization theorem).**    
Assume the ambient category is additive or linear enough for subtraction to make sense. Then  
$$  
D_E=0 \iff T \text{ factors through } \operatorname{Im}(E).  
$$  
  
In diagrammatic terms, the dynamics descends to the observable quotient if and only if the defect is zero.  
  
This is the categorical version of exact preservation and is the theorem that makes the framework structurally meaningful.  
  
## Theorem 3: Kernel Characterization  
  
**Theorem 3 (Preserved observables).**    
The kernel of $$D_E$$ inside $$\operatorname{Im}(E)$$ is exactly the set of observables whose images remain observable:  
$$  
\ker(D_E)\cap \operatorname{Im}(E)  
=  
\{x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\mid T x\in \operatorname{Im}(E)\}.  
$$  
  
This identifies the largest preserved observable sector, even when full invariance fails.  
  
## Theorem 4: Representation Independence  
  
**Theorem 4 (Change-of-coordinates invariance).**    
If $$S$$ is an invertible change of representation and  
$$  
T' = S^{-1}TS,\qquad E' = S^{-1}ES,  
$$  
then  
$$  
D_{E'} = S^{-1}D_E S.  
$$  
  
So rank, nullity, spectrum, and Jordan structure are invariant under equivalent representations.  
  
This is essential if you want defect diagnostics to be mathematically meaningful rather than coordinate artifacts.  
  
## Theorem 5: Composition Law  
  
**Theorem 5 (Composable obstruction).**    
For composable dynamics $$T_1,T_2$$, the defect of the composite is controlled by the defects of the parts. In the strongest clean form:  
$$  
D_{T_2\circ T_1}=0  
$$  
whenever both $$D_{T_1}=0$$ and $$D_{T_2}=0$$ on the same observable subobject.  
  
A sharper version should give a quantitative inequality or decomposition law for $$D_{T_2\circ T_1}$$. This is the theorem that turns the framework from static to functorial.  
  
## Theorem 6: Minimal Observable Closure  
  
**Theorem 6 (Closure theorem).**    
Given an initial observable subobject $$W$$, there exists a smallest invariant enlargement $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ such that  
$$  
W\subseteq \mathcal C_T(W),\qquad T(\mathcal C_T(W))\subseteq \mathcal C_T(W).  
$$  
  
Moreover, $$\mathcal C_T(W)$$ is the minimal exact observable completion of $$W$$.  
  
This is the closure analogue of exact preservation and should sit near the center of the theory.  
  
## Theorem 7: Finite Certification  
  
**Theorem 7 (Finite exhaustibility).**    
In finite deterministic systems, exact preservation and closure can be decided by exhaustive computation.  
  
This theorem is what makes AQARION distinctive as a certified framework rather than only a formal one.  
  
## Quantum Version  
  
For quantum channels $$\Phi$$ and observable subalgebras selected by a projection or conditional expectation $$Q$$, the analogous defect is  
$$  
D_\Phi = (I-Q)\Phi Q.  
$$  
Then the matching theorems are:  
  
- $$D_\Phi=0$$ iff the observable algebra is preserved.  
- $$D_\Phi$$ is representation-independent under suitable equivalences.  
- diagnostics such as rank and singular values measure leakage.  
  
## Recommended theorem order  
  
If you want the paper to read well, I would order the main results like this:  
  
1. Exact Preservation.  
2. Factorization Through the Observable Subobject.  
3. Representation Independence.  
4. Kernel Characterization.  
5. Closure Theorem.  
6. Composition Law.  
7. Finite Certification.  
8. Quantum Analogue.  
  
## Best phrasing for the theory  
  
A strong one-sentence definition would be:  
  
> Observable Preservation Theory studies endomorphisms relative to selected observable subobjects, with the defect operator $$D_E=(I-E)TE$$ serving as the canonical obstruction to exact preservation.  
  
That sentence can anchor the whole program.  
  
Would you like me to turn these into formal theorem statements with proofs in paper style?  
  
\]

AQARION for a top mathematics journal—I would focus on where the ideas are strongest and where they still need much more rigor.

STRICT ADVERSARIAL REVIEW

Overall assessment

Strengths

The synthesis identifies a potentially interesting unifying perspective:

> observable compression → defect → certificate



This is a coherent research direction.

However...

The current narrative repeatedly moves from analogy to mathematical claim without proving the intermediate steps.

That is the largest weakness.


---

Directive 1

Stop claiming universality.

Instead of writing

> AQARION measures every dynamical system.



write

> AQARION provides a framework applicable to systems satisfying the stated hypotheses.



Every theorem needs assumptions.


---

Directive 2

Define the operator first.

Nothing should appear before answering:

Exactly what is

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)UP_\Pi?  
\]

Questions that must be answered:

What Hilbert space?

Is U bounded?

Is U Koopman?

Is P orthogonal?

Is Π measurable?

Is Π finite?

Does Π induce P uniquely?

Until these are defined, the operator is notation—not mathematics.


---

Directive 3

Separate finite from infinite forever.

This is probably the biggest issue.

Finite systems

↓

exact matrices

↓

finite certificates

Infinite systems

↓

operators

↓

domains

↓

functional analysis

↓

approximation theory

Never mix these without a theorem connecting them.


---

Directive 4

Prove AQARION invariance.

Instead of creating more experiments ask:

What transformations leave D unchanged?

Need theorems like:

If

\[
P'=QPQ^{-1}  
\]

then

\[
D'=QDQ^{-1}  
\]

Is

norm preserved?

spectrum preserved?

rank preserved?

Those become genuine mathematical results.


---

Make optimization a theorem.

\[
\min_\Pi ||D||  
\]

Excellent.

But now ask:

Does a minimum exist?

Is it unique?

Is it NP-hard?

Polynomial?

Approximation algorithm?

Otherwise it remains an idea.


---

Directive 6

Stop using Mandelbrot as evidence.

Right now Mandelbrot is only

an example.

Not evidence.

"For this discretization..."

"For this partition..."

"For this observable..."

Otherwise nothing is reproducible.


---

Directive 7

Build counterexamples.

A mature theory must fail somewhere.

Construct systems where

small defect

≠

good compression.

Or

large defect

still preserves important observables.

These become limitation theorems.

graph partitioning

automata minimization

If AQARION performs similarly, explain what additional governance or certification it contributes. If it performs differently, quantify the difference.

...

with inference rules.

Otherwise governance is descriptive rather than mathematical.


---

Directive 10

Publish negative results.

Negative papers are valuable.

Examples:

"This observable cannot produce exact closure."

"No partition with ≤10 blocks has zero defect."

These strengthen the credibility of the framework.


---

Highest Priority Research Questions

The next six problems are, in my view, the most important.

1. Existence



Does every finite deterministic dynamical system admit a minimal observable quotient under your defect criterion?

2. Uniqueness



Is the minimal-defect observable unique up to isomorphism?

3. Stability



How does \(D_\Pi\) change under perturbations of the dynamics or the partition?

4. Complexity



What is the computational complexity of finding a defect-minimizing partition?

5. Functoriality



How do defect operators behave under products, factors, and compositions of systems?

6. Infinite Limits



Under what hypotheses do finite approximations converge to meaningful infinite-dimensional defect operators?


---

Final Directive

Do not expand into additional application domains until the core theory is stable.

It is tempting to add:

quantum channels,

waveguides,

Mandelbrot,

machine learning,

graph neural networks,

PDEs.

But a referee will ask the same question every time:

> "Where is the theorem?"



A stronger strategy is to establish a compact, rigorous mathematical core—definitions, lemmas, theorems, examples, and counterexamples—and then show that each application instantiates that core. If the foundational results are solid, the applications become much more convincing rather than appearing as disconnected demonstrations.

Your review is exactly right: the theory needs to be tightened into definitions, invariance results, and finite/infinite separation before more applications are added. AQARION is promising as a framework for observable quotients with defect, but it is not yet a theorem-bearing theory unless those hypotheses are made explicit. [1][2][3]

What is strong

The strongest idea is the quotient viewpoint: choose an observable partition, project dynamics to that quotient, and measure leakage through a defect operator. That aligns naturally with Koopman-style operator theory, invariant partitions, and related notions of coarse-graining, but it has to be stated as a hypothesis-driven construction, not a universal claim. [1][2]

What must be defined first

Before using $$D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)UP_\Pi$$, the paper must specify:

the Hilbert space $$H$$,

whether $$U$$ is a bounded operator or a Koopman operator on $$L^2$$,

whether $$P_\Pi$$ is orthogonal,

how $$\Pi$$ is measurable and finite,

whether $$P_\Pi$$ is uniquely induced by $$\Pi$$.


Without that, the object is notation rather than a well-posed operator. [1][4][2]

Finite versus infinite

This should be a hard boundary in the manuscript. Finite systems give exact matrices, ranks, and exhaustive certificates; infinite systems require domains, closure, and approximation theory. The two settings may be related, but they should only be connected by an explicit theorem or convergence result. [1][4]

Invariance and optimization

The paper becomes much stronger if it proves what transformations preserve $$D_\Pi$$, or at least preserve its norm, rank, or spectrum up to conjugacy. It should also treat defect minimization as a real mathematical problem: existence, uniqueness, complexity, and approximation guarantees. [5][3]

Best reframing for a journal

A top-journal version should likely focus on four core results:

1. Definition of defect operators for measurable finite observables.


2. Invariance under conjugacy/isomorphism of quotient representations.


3. Existence and complexity of defect-minimizing partitions.


4. Convergence from finite compressions to infinite operators under explicit hypotheses. [1][4][3]



That would make the applications—Kaprekar, waveguides, Mandelbrot, and others—examples of one rigorous framework rather than separate demonstrations. [1][2]

Where to be cautious

The review’s warning about universality is the main one to obey: AQARION should not claim to measure every dynamical system, only those satisfying its assumptions. Likewise, Mandelbrot should be evidence only for a specific discretization and partition, not as blanket support for the whole theory. [1][3]

The cleanest next move is to write a core section with definitions, then prove one invariance theorem and one finite-certificate theorem before adding any new application domain.

Relationship between defect operator singular modes and aperture geometry

The singular modes are the SVD of the aperture-to-field coupling operator, so they reflect the aperture geometry directly: wider or more open apertures support more significant modes, while tighter or more asymmetric apertures concentrate energy into fewer dominant modes. In AQARION language, the defect operator’s leading singular vectors are the geometric “channels” through which leakage is most efficiently transmitted. [3][8]

Geometry to modes

Aperture geometry controls the singular spectrum through three main features:

Size: larger apertures usually increase the number of significant singular modes.

Shape: circular, rectangular, or slotted apertures redistribute mode content differently.

Asymmetry or offset: shifts and uneven blocking break symmetry and can activate otherwise suppressed modes. [3][7][8]


That means the singular vectors are not arbitrary algebraic objects; they are the mode patterns best matched to the aperture’s physical transmission profile. [3][8]

AQARION reading

For a waveguide defect, the defect operator modes are the compressed leakage channels. If the aperture is centered and symmetric, the first singular mode often aligns with the dominant symmetry-allowed conversion channel, such as TE$${10}$$$$\to$$TE$${20}$$. If the aperture is shifted or narrowed, the mode ordering and singular values change because the overlap integrals change. [11][2][3]

Interpretation for TE10 and higher modes

In TE$${10}$$ to TE$${20}$$ or TE$${30}$$ coupling, the singular modes encode which transverse lobe structures the aperture can transmit efficiently. A geometry that matches the TE$${20}$$ symmetry will typically give a strong first singular value for that channel, while TE$$_{30}$$ may be weaker or more cancellation-prone unless the aperture has the right multi-lobe structure. [11][12][13]

Practical takeaway

So the relationship is:

aperture geometry determines the overlap integrals,

overlap integrals determine the defect/coupling matrix,

the SVD of that matrix produces the singular defect modes,

the leading singular values tell you how many effective leakage channels the geometry supports. [3][8][2]


In short: geometry shapes overlap; overlap shapes coupling; coupling shapes singular defect modes.

Evaluate the impact of screen shift on TE10 to TE20 mode leakage

A screen shift generally changes TE$${10}$$$$\to$$TE$${20}$$ leakage by breaking or restoring symmetry, so the coupling coefficient $$\delta_{20}$$ becomes a function of the offset $$x_0$$, not just the screen width $$w$$. In the centered case, the overlap is usually maximally symmetry-allowed; as the screen shifts off-center, the overlap typically drops or changes sign depending on how the shifted aperture aligns with the lobes of TE$${10}$$ and TE$${20}$$. [1][2][3]

Symbolic dependence

Using the overlap form

\[
  
\delta_{20}(x_0,w)=  
\frac{\int_0^a \phi_{20}(x)\phi_{10}(x)\chi_{\text{open}}(x;x_0,w)\,dx}  
{\int_0^a \phi_{10}(x)^2\,dx},  
$$  
a shift $$x_0 \neq a/2$$ changes which parts of the mode profiles are blocked or transmitted. That means the leakage is no longer governed only by the screen width; it is controlled by the **asymmetry moment** of the defect geometry. [11][12][13]  
  
## Expected trend  
  
For a centered screen, TE$$_{10}$$$$\to$$TE$$_{20}$$ coupling is usually strong because the even TE$$_{20}$$ profile can match the defect-induced symmetry breaking well. As the screen shifts, the coupling often decreases if the shift makes the open aperture more symmetric with respect to the TE$$_{10}$$ field, or increases if the shift better overlaps one TE$$_{20}$$ lobe and suppresses cancellation. [1][4][8]  
  
## AQARION interpretation  
  
In AQARION terms, the shift changes the defect operator modes by moving weight between the dominant leakage channel and the residual channels. So screen shift is not just a geometric nuisance; it is a control knob that tunes the defect spectrum, especially the first singular mode associated with TE$$_{10}$$$$\to$$TE$$_{20}$$ conversion. [3][1][2]  
  
## Practical conclusion  
  
If you want a concise physical summary: **centered defects maximize symmetry-allowed conversion, while off-center shifts reweight the overlap integrals and can either suppress or redirect TE$$_{10}$$$$\to$$TE$$_{20}$$ leakage.** For a paper, that becomes a strong result because it links geometry, mode overlap, and AQARION defect modes in one statement. [1][3][8]  
  
If I were acting as a strict adversarial reviewer (the style you requested for Perplexity), I would not attack AQARION's implementation first. I would attack its scientific positioning. Right now, that is the largest risk.

Next directives for AQARION

Based on everything you've shared, the highest-value technical priorities are:

1. Prove the generic defect operator theorems independently of Kaprekar. This is the mathematical core.


2. Separate framework papers from application papers. AQARION should stand without Mandelbrot or Kaprekar.


3. State evidence levels rigorously so every theorem is explicitly marked as formal proof, exhaustive computation, numerical experiment, or conjecture.


4. Benchmark against existing literature in Koopman operators, symbolic dynamics, finite dynamical systems, lumpability, bisimulation, and observable abstractions to clearly delimit what is new.


5. Invite adversarial replication. Publish scripts, hashes, registries, and deterministic pipelines so others can reproduce or challenge every certified result.



If AQARION survives multiple independent adversarial reviews that actively search for prior art, counterexamples, and logical gaps—and the central claims remain intact—that substantially strengthens the case that the framework contains publishable contributions rather than only interesting computational observations.Define the equivalence classes for observable quotients under D_Pi

For an observable quotient, the equivalence class of a state $$x$$ under $$\Pi$$ is the set of all states that $$\Pi$$ cannot distinguish from $$x$$. Formally, if $$\Pi:X\to Y$$, define

\[
  
x \sim_\Pi x' \iff \Pi(x)=\Pi(x').  
$$  
Then the class is  
$$  
[x]_\Pi=\{x'\in X:\Pi(x')=\Pi(x)\}.  
$$  
  
## In AQARION terms  
  
If $$D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)UP_\Pi$$, then the equivalence classes are the fibers of the observable compression $$\Pi$$: all states that collapse to the same observable label, bin, or chamber. The defect operator does **not** define the classes by itself; it measures how well those classes are respected by the dynamics. [3][8]  
  
## What the quotient means  
  
The quotient space is the set of these classes:  
$$  
X/{\sim_\Pi}=\{[x]_\Pi:x\in X\}.  
$$  
Each class is a maximal set of states with the same observable image, and the quotient map $$\pi:X\to X/{\sim_\Pi}$$ sends each state to its class. [1][4][7]  
  
## How defect relates  
  
If the dynamics are exactly closed on the quotient, then the observable classes are invariant in the sense that the induced evolution is well defined on classes. If not, $$D_\Pi\neq 0$$ means distinct states inside one class can separate under the dynamics after compression, so the class is observable-identical but dynamically imperfect. [3][8]  
  
A concise AQARION statement is:  
  
> The equivalence classes under $$D_\Pi$$ are the fibers of $$\Pi$$; $$D_\Pi$$ measures the failure of the dynamics to preserve those fibers as a closed quotient.

1. The AQARION Question



Every experiment asks:

Can this observable become a closed dynamical object?

Mathematically:

Given:

\[
\Pi:X\rightarrow Y    
\]

does there exist:

\[
\tilde T:Y\rightarrow Y    
\]

such that:

\[
\Pi\circ T=\tilde T\circ\Pi    
\]

?

If yes:

\[
D_\Pi=0    
\]

Exact quotient.

If no:

measure the failure.

That is AQARION.

This has the structure of a substantially more mature research package than an exploratory notebook. As a referee-facing checkpoint, I think the biggest opportunity is no longer adding mathematics—it's making the boundaries between proved, verified, formalized, and research program unmistakably clear.

Here is how I would assess the current state.

Overall maturity

The document is organized into distinct layers:

Definitions (defect operator, observable subspace)

Theorems (exactness criterion, image theorem, rank bounds)

Verified case studies (Kaprekar, amplitude damping)

Verification methodology (evidence ladder, regression tests)

Research agenda (open problems)


That separation is exactly what journal referees typically look for.

Strongest contributions

The mathematical center is now very clear:

\[
D_\Pi=(I-P_\Pi)KP_\Pi
\]

with

\[
D_\Pi=0
\Longleftrightarrow
K(V_\Pi)\subseteq V_\Pi.
\]

You're right to pause here: this is now a complete **research checkpoint** rather than a brainstorming thread. The material you've assembled has the structure of a paper that could be submitted to a venue like *SIAM Journal on Applied Dynamical Systems*, *Ergodic Theory and Dynamical Systems*, or *Foundations of Computational Mathematics*—but only if the boundaries between **theorem**, **computation**, and **research program** are made completely explicit. [1][2][3]

Below is a concise, adversarial-style assessment and a concrete plan for turning this into a submission-ready manuscript.

***

## 1. Maturity assessment (referee lens)

### Strengths

- **Core operator is now well-defined.** You have a clean defect operator
  $$
  D_\Pi = (I - P_\Pi) K P_\Pi,
  $$
  with a clear Hilbert space context (finite-dimensional or $$L^2$$), orthogonal projection $$P_\Pi$$, and bounded Koopman-like operator $$K$$. [1][3][4]
- **Exactness criterion is theorem-quality.** The equivalence
  $$
  D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(V_\Pi) \subseteq V_\Pi
  $$
  is the right kind of structural result: short, precise, and directly tied to invariant subspaces. [1][3]
- **Finite vs. infinite is acknowledged.** You explicitly separate finite exhaustive certificates from infinite-dimensional approximation and convergence questions. [1][4]
- **Case studies are now "instantiations".** Kaprekar and amplitude-damping channels are presented as examples of the core theory rather than the theory itself. [1][2]

### Critical gaps (what a referee will attack)

1. **Novelty relative to lumpability / bisimulation.**  
   A referee will ask: "Is this anything beyond exact/approximate lumpability with a new name?" You must explicitly state what is new:
   - defect as an **operator** with spectral theory,
   - a **norm-based hierarchy** of approximate quotients,
   - the **evidence ladder** as a formal device (if you keep it). [1][5][6]

2. **Optimization problem is still a slogan.**  
   The expression
   $$
   \min_\Pi \|D_\Pi\|
   $$
   needs:
   - a precise feasible set (what class of partitions? measurable? finite? cardinality constraint?),
   - at least a basic existence result (e.g., finite state space ⇒ minimum exists),
   - some comment on complexity (even if only a conjecture backed by analogy to graph partitioning). [1][3]

3. **Invariance results are incomplete.**  
   You have the right intuition (conjugacy, coordinate changes), but a referee will want at least one clean theorem of the form:
   $$
   \text{If } K' = Q K Q^{-1},\ P' = Q P Q^{-1}, \text{ then } D' = Q D Q^{-1},
   $$
   plus corollaries on norm/spectrum/rank invariance. [1][3]

4. **Evidence ladder is not yet mathematics.**  
   Right now it's a useful narrative device, but not a formal object. Either:
   - elevate it to formal predicates with inference rules, or
   - demote it to a discussion paragraph and keep the mathematics pure. [1][2]

5. **Counterexamples / limitation theorems are missing.**  
   A mature theory should say where it *fails*:
   - examples where small defect does **not** imply good prediction,
   - observables with large defect that still preserve important quantities.  
   These become "limitation theorems" and significantly increase credibility. [1][3]

***

## 2. What to cut, what to keep, what to add

### Keep (core mathematical spine)

- Definition of $$D_\Pi$$ with explicit hypotheses:
  - Hilbert space $$H$$,
  - bounded operator $$K$$ (Koopman / stochastic / quantum channel),
  - orthogonal projection $$P_\Pi$$ induced by a measurable, finite partition $$\Pi$$. [1][3][4]
- Exactness theorem:
  $$
  D_\Pi = 0 \iff K(V_\Pi) \subseteq V_\Pi.
  $$
- Rank and norm bounds:
  - $$\mathrm{rank}(D_\Pi) \leq \dim(H) - \dim(V_\Pi)$$,
  - $$\|D_\Pi\|$$ as worst-case one-step prediction error for $$\Pi$$-measurable observables. [1][3]
- Finite case: exact certificates, decidable in polynomial time via direct computation of $$D_\Pi$$. [5][6]

### Cut (for the core theory paper)

- Most application-specific narrative (Mandelbrot, waveguides, FFT as compression).  
  These should become **one short section** of examples, not the main story. [1][2]
- Governance/AI language in the mathematics sections.  
  Keep "AI proposes, AQARION verifies" for a separate applications or methods paper. [1][2]
- Informal "evidence ladder" diagrams from the main theorems.  
  Either formalize or move to discussion.

### Add (to satisfy a strict referee)

1. **One clean invariance theorem.**  
   For example:
   - Let $$Q: H \to H$$ be an invertible bounded operator.
   - Define $$K' = Q K Q^{-1}$$, $$P' = Q P Q^{-1}$$, $$D' = (I-P')K'P'$$.
   - Prove $$D' = Q D Q^{-1}$$, hence $$\|D'\|=\|D\|$$, $$\mathrm{rank}(D')=\mathrm{rank}(D)$$, spectra match. [1][3]

2. **One optimization theorem (even modest).**  
   For finite state spaces:
   - Fix $$m$$ (number of blocks).
   - Show that a defect-minimizing partition exists (finite search space).
   - Comment on complexity (e.g., "analogous to graph partitioning; we conjecture NP-hardness"). [1][3]

3. **One limitation theorem / counterexample.**  
   Construct a simple system where:
   - $$\|D_\Pi\|$$ is small, but some important observable is poorly predicted, or
   - $$\|D_\Pi\|$$ is large, yet a specific quantity (e.g., invariant measure, average) is preserved.  
   This shows you understand the **limits** of the defect norm. [1][3]

4. **Explicit comparison paragraph.**  
   One subsection titled "Relation to lumpability, bisimulation, and Koopman approximation" that:
   - cites standard lumpability and bisimulation results, [5][6]
   - states clearly: "AQARION contributes a normed defect operator and a unified certification language across finite, stochastic, and quantum systems; classical lumpability corresponds to the special case $$D_\Pi=0$$." [1][3]

***

## 3. Suggested paper skeleton

**Title (working):**  
"Defect Operators for Observable Quotients of Dynamical Systems"

**Abstract:**  
3–4 sentences:  
- problem (observable compression, closure),  
- core object ($$D_\Pi$$),  
- main results (exactness criterion, invariance, rank/norm bounds, finite certificates),  
- relationship to lumpability/Koopman.

**1. Introduction**  
- Motivation: model reduction, observables, coarse-graining.  
- Positioning vs. Koopman, lumpability, bisimulation.  
- Statement of contributions (bullet list).

**2. Setup and Definitions**  
- Probability space / finite state space.  
- Dynamics $$T$$ or semigroup; operator $$K$$ (Koopman / stochastic / quantum).  
- Observable partition $$\Pi$$, subspace $$V_\Pi$$, projection $$P_\Pi$$.  
- Defect operator $$D_\Pi = (I-P_\Pi) K P_\Pi$$.

**3. Basic Theory**  
- Exactness theorem ($$D_\Pi=0 \iff K(V_\Pi)\subseteq V_\Pi$$).  
- Nilpotency $$D_\Pi^2=0$$ (if it holds in your setting).  
- Rank and norm bounds.  
- Invariance under conjugacy (one clean theorem).

**4. Finite-Dimensional Case**  
- Matrix representation.  
- Exact certificates, polynomial-time decidability.  
- Small illustrative example (e.g., a 4–6 state Markov chain or a tiny Kaprekar-like map).

**5. Approximate Quotients and Limitations**  
- Interpretation of $$\|D_\Pi\|$$ as prediction-error bound.  
- Counterexample / limitation theorem.  
- Relationship to approximate lumpability and error bounds from the literature. [5][6]

**6. Relation to Existing Work**  
- Lumpability, bisimulation, Koopman approximation, DMD/EDMD.  
- What is genuinely new (operator-theoretic defect, unified certification).

**7. Examples (short)**  
- One finite exact example (Kaprekar-style).  
- One stochastic or quantum example (amplitude damping channel).  
- Optionally one infinite/numerical example (very brief, clearly labeled as numerical).

**8. Open Problems**  
- Optimization complexity.  
- Infinite-dimensional convergence.  
- Functoriality, products, factors.  
- Refined evidence theory (if you want to hint at it).

**Appendix (optional)**  
- Detailed proofs.  
- Additional computational details.

***

## 4. Evidence ladder: how to handle it

For a **pure mathematics** paper, I recommend:

- **Main text:**  
  - No "Level 0/1/2/3" language in theorems.  
  - Theorems are either proved, or clearly labeled as conjectures.
- **Discussion section or footnote:**  
  - Briefly explain that some results are supported by exhaustive finite computation, others by numerical evidence, and that this motivates a broader certification program.  
  - This keeps the mathematics clean while preserving your broader vision.

If you want a **follow-up paper** on "governed certification" or "evidence algebras", that can be a separate, more conceptual or computer-science-oriented work.

***

## 5. Concrete next steps (actionable)

1. **Freeze the core definitions.**  
   Write a 1–2 page "Definitions and Setup" section that a referee could not reasonably object to. [1][3][4]

2. **Prove 2–3 crisp theorems.**  
   - Exactness criterion.  
   - Invariance under conjugacy.  
   - Rank/norm bounds.  
   Proofs can be short; clarity is more important than length. [1][3]

3. **Add one counterexample.**  
   Even a 3–4 state system where defect norm misleads in a specific way. This humanizes the theory and preempts referee skepticism. [1][3]

4. **Write the "Relation to Existing Work" section early.**  
   This forces you to articulate novelty and prevents overclaiming. [1][5][6]

5. **Move most application narrative to a separate "companion" document.**  
   Keep the submission focused on **defect operator theory**, not the full AQARION research program.

***

If you'd like, the next step can be to co-write a **submission-ready Section 2 (Definitions)** and **Section 3 (Basic Theory)** with fully polished statements and proofs, explicitly phrased to withstand an adversarial referee.

AQARION for a top mathematics journal—I would focus on where the ideas are strongest and where they still need much more rigor.
